section_path: FSM Input for the Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act

paragraphs.text: The FSM is a German state-approved self-regulatory body for digital services and online media. Amongst our members are some of the Very Large Online Platforms as well as a range of video on demand service providers from across Europe. Since 1997, the FSM has been working to ensure that children and young people can grow up with a safer and better Internet - in particular by combating illegal and harmful content online. To this end, the FSM operates an Internet Hotline that anyone can contact to report online content. In addition, the FSM does extensive educational work and promotes media literacy skills among children, young people and adults.

section_path: FSM Input for the Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act

evidence_quotes.quote: certain marketing and advertising measures are specifically aimed at them.

paragraphs.text: As an organization dedicated to youth protection, we believe that consumer protection regulations specifically targeting children and young people are important. This is particularly true given that minors are a vulnerable group in the context of consumer law, yet certain marketing and advertising measures are specifically aimed at them. This makes special protection necessary.

section_path: FSM Input for the Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act

paragraphs.text: We also welcome the DFA's goal of clarifying previous ambiguities due to the fragmentation of the legal situation. Nevertheless, it is essential to ensure that no further regulations are created that could potentially counteract existing options.

section_path: FSM Input for the Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act

paragraphs.text: New regulations should only be introduced where genuine gaps in protection have been identified. Furthermore, we consider it very important that the planned DFA regulations relating to the protection of minors do not conflict with existing and already implemented rules (e.g. according to the AVMSD) or rules that may not yet have been implemented (e.g. DSA). The rules in Article 28 DSA and the associated Guidelines already contain extensive provisions, including those relating to the consumer rights of young people. Even though the DSA and the guidelines only apply to online platforms as defined by the DSA, there are naturally many areas of overlap. For example, the DFA is also intends to regulate influencer marketing on platforms, which is also covered by the DSA and Guidelines.

section_path: 1. Avoiding conflicts with existing rules and further fragmentation

paragraphs.text: Since the Guidelines were only issued in mid-2025 and neither providers nor regulators have had the opportunity to implement and evaluate the options regarding their practical suitability, great care should be taken to avoid creating parallel legislation and obligations in this area. It might be advisable to analyse how the measures set out in the Guidelines work in practice first before deciding on any further specific provisions or legal consequences. This would be aligned with the current simplification agenda of the EU Commission and the better regulation principle.

section_path: 1. Avoiding conflicts with existing rules and further fragmentation

section_path: 1. Avoiding conflicts with existing rules and further fragmentation
1. Avoiding conflicts with existing rules and further fragmentation

paragraphs.text: Another example would be the regulation of "Dark Patterns" which are already covered by several EU legal provisions and are expressly prohibited in Article 25 of the DSA.

section_path: 1. Avoiding conflicts with existing rules and further fragmentation

paragraphs.text: The DFA should avoid fragmentation of regulations at all costs; instead it should focus on its declared objective to clarify existing regulations where such clarification has been neglected thus far.

section_path: 1. Avoiding conflicts with existing rules and further fragmentation

evidence_quotes.quote: The creation of the DFA offers the opportunity to encourage service providers to integrate references to independent counselling services into their offerings.
Linking independent voluntary self-regulation bodies in the field of minor protection and advice centres could be one item on a list of possible suggestions for making consumer offerings safer.

paragraphs.text: The creation of the DFA offers the opportunity to encourage service providers to integrate references to independent counselling services into their offerings. Linking independent voluntary self-regulation bodies in the field of minor protection and advice centres could be one item on a list of possible suggestions for making consumer offerings safer.

section_path: 1. Avoiding conflicts with existing rules and further fragmentation
2. Consideration of specialized reporting centres / hotlines

paragraphs.text: For example, services that create a particular risk by making gambling-like mechanisms available could be required to integrate links to counselling and addiction prevention centres into their services. The necessity of implementing such measures could be determined by conducting a risk assessment as any new specific provisions should be risk-based and focused on the highest-risk services/features to complement the DSA Guidelines. An introduction of blanket measures should be avoided in any case.

section_path: 1. Avoiding conflicts with existing rules and further fragmentation
2. Consideration of specialized reporting centres / hotlines

paragraphs.text: It is crucial that the perspective of children and young people is taken into account in the further development of the law – both through specific protection requirements and through the promotion of media literacy and digital resilience. In order to adequately reflect this perspective, direct involvement of the young target group in the run-up to the legislative process is recommended.

section_path: 1. Avoiding conflicts with existing rules and further fragmentation
3. Involving minors in the ongoing development process
